summary: The case describes post-bereavement numbness with cue-triggered distress, labile mood, and work-use impairment. The proposed predictors are modifiable BIO/PSYCHO/SOCIAL levers designed to (a) stabilize sleep and circadian functioning, (b) promote active grief processing and meaning-making, (c) reduce cue-reactivity through grounding and desensitization, (d) optimize work concentration via CBT-informed structuring, (e) strengthen validating social support, (f) foster identity/meaning integration, and (g) leverage exercise and mindfulness to modulate affect and cognitive function. Each lever is aimed at specific criteria (C01-C06) and includes simple, scalable measurements and feasible time horizons.

solutions.solution_candidate: Implement a structured sleep-wake schedule with consistent wake time, morning light exposure (10–20 minutes), evening dim-light routines, and reduced screen exposure 1 hour before bed. Use a daily sleep diary and optional actigraphy to track duration, consistency, and awakenings; adjust timing to optimize daytime alertness.
solutions.mechanism_rationale: Regular sleep supports emotional regulation and cognitive control, reducing reactivity to cues and improving concentration.
solutions.measurement: Sleep diary (bedtime/wake time, awakenings), weekly Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI) score, daytime functioning rating (0–10).
solutions.mapping_query: bereavement sleep stabilization sleep hygiene cognitive impact on grief numbness

solutions.solution_candidate: Structured grief processing plan (brief grief-focused CBT or ACT-informed processing) plus journaling and facilitated memory-sharing with a supportive listener; incorporate values/meaning-making exercises to re-engage with life. Escalate to trauma-informed approaches if distress remains high.
solutions.mechanism_rationale: Active processing and meaning-making reduce avoidance, reactivity to reminders, and numbness by reintegrating loss into personal narrative.
solutions.measurement: Grief engagement minutes/day, daily distress cue log, brief grief adaptation scale (e.g., PG-13 or a brief 5-item measure).
solutions.mapping_query: grief processing meaning making bereavement CBT journaling

solutions.solution_candidate: Teach grounding techniques (5-4-3-2-1; paced breathing; progressive muscle relaxation) and a controlled, gradual exposure/venting plan for common cues (smell, music) to reduce episodic distress and reactivity. Maintain a cue-management log and practice plans.
solutions.mechanism_rationale: Grounding and paced exposure reduce autonomic arousal and help regain sense of present-mowler during cue-triggered episodes, decreasing numbness over time.
solutions.measurement: Number of cue-triggered episodes per week, subjective distress (0–10) at peak episodes, daily grounding practice minutes.
solutions.mapping_query: cue exposure grounding grief distress tolerance

solutions.solution_candidate: Implement work accommodations: time-blocking, prioritized task lists, 25–50 minute focused blocks with 5-minute breaks, environmental tweaks, and brief mindfulness micro-practices before task starts. Use a simple progress-tracking sheet or app to monitor focus and productivity.
solutions.mechanism_rationale: Structured work routines reduce cognitive load from distress and improve sustained attention during tasks.
solutions.measurement: Average focus duration per task block, task completion rate, self-rated focus 0–10, supervisor/peer feedback if available.
solutions.mapping_query: work concentration grief cognitive load management

solutions.solution_candidate: Enhance access to empathetic social support: identify a trusted listener, peer support group, or therapist; train close others in validating responses and avoiding dismissive platitudes; consider social prescribing or structured check-ins to combat perceived minimization.
solutions.mechanism_rationale: VALIDATING social interactions buffer distress, reduce social withdrawal, and lessen numbness by restoring social belonging.
solutions.measurement: Perceived social support scale (e.g., MSPSS) scores; frequency of supportive conversations; sense of belonging rating (0–10).
solutions.mapping_query: compassionate listening bereavement social support training

solutions.solution_candidate: Engage in identity- and meaning-focused activities: values clarification, narrative reconstruction of self post-loss, and activities aligned with personal values to rebuild purpose. Use brief guidance on integrating loss into identity.
solutions.mechanism_rationale: Reinstating a coherent sense of self and purpose reduces existential distress and promotes re-engagement with life.
solutions.measurement: Daily values-aligned activity log; meaning-in-life questionnaire scores; grief adaptation scale.
solutions.mapping_query: grief processing meaning making identity integration

solutions.solution_candidate: Introduce regular aerobic activity (e.g., 30 minutes brisk walking most days) with a simple plan, plus short movement breaks during work. Track via steps or minutes and pair with social options (walks with a friend) when feasible.
solutions.mechanism_rationale: Exercise enhances mood, cognitive function, and stress resilience, aiding concentration and reducing distress reactivity.
solutions.measurement: Weekly minutes of activity, daily step count, mood ratings (0–10) pre/post activity, work focus metrics.

solutions.solution_candidate: Provide brief mindfulness-based skill-building (daily 5–10 minute practices, guided audio) focusing on present-moment awareness and emotion regulation; integrate with daily routines and grief processing.
solutions.mechanism_rationale: Mindfulness improves regulation of affect and reduces reactivity to grief cues, ironing out numbness and rapid shifts.
solutions.measurement: Daily mindfulness practice minutes; Five Facet Mindfulness Questionnaire (FFMQ) or Mindful Attention Awareness Scale (MAAS) scores; distress and numbness ratings (0–10).
